summary:
The earliest known papyrus scrolls in the archaeological record date to approximately 2600–2500 BC and were discovered at the Wadi al-Jarf site on the Red Sea coast of Egypt. These documents, unearthed in 2013, include logs written by an official named Merer who supervised teams transporting limestone blocks to the Giza plateau during the construction of the [[event:1708|Great Pyramid of Giza]]. They are the oldest known papyrus documents and provide direct, contemporary evidence for the organization of pyramid construction labor — previously a subject of considerable scholarly debate.

Before these discoveries, papyrus was known primarily from somewhat later specimens. The Wadi al-Jarf papyri demonstrate that roll-form papyrus documents were already a standard administrative tool during the Fourth Dynasty, suggesting the technology had likely been developed earlier, during or before the Third Dynasty. Papyrus manufacture, which involved cutting, soaking, layering, and pressing strips from the pith of the Cyperus papyrus plant, required specialized knowledge and became one of ancient Egypt's most important technologies, enabling the sophisticated record-keeping systems that supported Egyptian governance, trade, and religious practice for millennia.
